title: China’s De-Dollarization Drive Has Hit a Wall
gist: Beijing has built real de-dollarization infrastructure — renminbi trade settlement, the CIPS clearing system, and the e-CNY digital currency — but genuine global demand for Chinese financial instruments remains marginal because capital controls, thin correspondent-bank networks, and low incentives choke adoption; dollar-pegged stablecoins, not the yuan, are capturing the real emerging-market appetite for alternative digital money.

insights.claim: China's de-dollarization campaign has produced genuine financial infrastructure but has not translated into influence, because resilience (having alternative channels) is not the same as demand (others willingly using them).
insights.speaker: Agathe Demarais
insights.so_what: The core investable distinction: China's optionality against sanctions is real, but bet against near-term renminbi displacement of the dollar in global trade or reserves.
insights.quote: resilience is not the same as influence; few actors willingly adopt Chinese financial tools.
insights.locator: Over the past decade, Beijing has made genuine progress

insights.claim: Chinese firms now settle around 30% of their trade in renminbi, up from virtually zero 15 years ago, but globally fewer than 5% of transactions are settled in renminbi, and its use is almost exclusively confined to deals involving at least one Chinese firm.
insights.speaker: Agathe Demarais
insights.so_what: Quantifies the ceiling on renminbi internationalization — useful benchmark for tracking whether the 2026 PBOC push (offshore trading pilots, new swap lines) actually moves the needle.
insights.quote: renminbi use remains marginal, with fewer than 5 percent of transactions settled in the Chinese currency.

insights.claim: Beijing's own capital controls make it costly for foreign firms to hold renminbi: offshore yuan deposits stood at just $234 billion in early 2025, versus roughly $15 trillion in dollar-denominated assets held outside the United States.
insights.speaker: Agathe Demarais
insights.so_what: The offshore-yuan pool is two orders of magnitude smaller than offshore dollar assets — a hard capital-controls constraint that PBOC pilot schemes alone cannot fix without liberalizing the capital account.
insights.quote: a rounding error compared to the $15 trillion in dollar-denominated assets outside the United States.

insights.claim: Of the 1,791 banks China cites as part of its CIPS clearing network across 126 countries, only 194 are directly connected, and nearly all of those are Chinese; the non-Chinese core group is just two U.S. banks (Citibank, JPMorgan Chase), two Eurozone banks (BNP Paribas, Deutsche Bank), and three Japanese banks (Mizuho, MUFG, Sumitomo Mitsui).
insights.speaker: Agathe Demarais
insights.so_what: The headline '1,791 banks' figure vastly overstates real non-Chinese participation — a useful corrective when Chinese officials cite CIPS scale as evidence of sanctions resilience.
insights.quote: just 194 banks are directly connected to CIPS, while all the others access the scheme via intermediaries.
insights.locator: Take the claim about the 1,791 banks

insights.claim: About 80% of transactions that go through CIPS also route through Europe-based SWIFT for messaging, meaning CIPS functions as redundancy alongside Western payment rails rather than a genuine sanctions-proof alternative to them.
insights.speaker: Agathe Demarais
insights.so_what: Undercuts the thesis that CIPS gives China or its partners meaningful sanctions insulation today — a key input for handicapping China-Russia-Iran financial-workaround narratives.
insights.locator: This distinction matters, since only those banks

themes.theme: e-CNY's weak traction, at home and abroad

insights.claim: The digital yuan (e-CNY) has 225 million wallet holders and $2.3 trillion in cumulative transactions since its 2020 launch, but daily flow is only about $6 billion — versus more than $150 billion processed daily by Alipay and WeChat Pay combined.
insights.speaker: Agathe Demarais
insights.so_what: Even domestically, China's state-backed digital currency is a rounding error next to incumbent private payment apps — a poor proxy for renminbi digital dominance abroad.
insights.quote: Flows in e-CNY amount to a mere $6 billion per day
insights.locator: But once again, looking beyond the headline data

insights.claim: To force adoption, some Chinese localities now pay public-sector salaries in e-CNY, and accounts began earning 0.05% annual interest as of January 2026 — a rate too small to meaningfully drive uptake.
insights.speaker: Agathe Demarais
insights.so_what: Signals policymaker frustration with slow e-CNY takeup and that state compulsion, not market appeal, is the main adoption lever left.
insights.quote: interest payments are unlikely to fuel a rush toward Beijing’s digital currency.
insights.locator: In a sure sign that slow takeup is proving frustrating

insights.claim: The cross-border mBridge digital-currency platform, running since roughly 2022, has attracted only four members (Hong Kong, Saudi Arabia, Thailand, UAE) and processes about four payments a day worth roughly $50 million — about 0.0005% of total global FX turnover — and became further stigmatized after the Bank for International Settlements exited the project in 2024 over sanctions-circumvention concerns.
insights.speaker: Agathe Demarais
insights.so_what: The most sanctions-relevant cross-border rail China has backed is now reputationally 'toxic' post-BIS exit — a headwind for any China/Russia/Iran plan to route around dollar-based settlement via mBridge.
insights.quote: The decision from the Bank for International Settlements to exit the project in 2024 amid controversies around its potential use for sanctions circumvention made the project toxic
insights.locator: The decision from the Bank for International Settlements

insights.claim: Undeterred by mBridge's stall, Beijing announced in mid-June 2026 a new round-the-clock scheme, the Cross-border e-CNY Transfer Services (CBETS), with 26 institutions signed up so far.
insights.speaker: Agathe Demarais
insights.so_what: A new vehicle to watch for whether Chinese cross-border digital-currency adoption inflects upward in 2026-2027, or repeats mBridge's pattern of shallow uptake.
insights.locator: Beijing keeps creating further schemes regardless

themes.theme: Stablecoins are winning the digital-money race China wants

insights.claim: Global demand for alternative digital money is real, but it is flowing to dollar-pegged stablecoins, not the yuan: the stablecoin market has grown from zero to $317 billion over six years, overwhelmingly pegged to the dollar.
insights.speaker: Agathe Demarais
insights.so_what: Dollar dominance in digital form is compounding even as China pushes de-dollarization — a bullish signal for dollar-stablecoin issuers and a structural headwind for renminbi internationalization.
insights.quote: The stablecoin market has grown from zero to $317 billion over the past six years
insights.locator: Perhaps the hardest bit to swallow for Chinese policymakers

insights.claim: Two-thirds of global stablecoins are held by individuals in emerging markets prone to currency instability and capital controls (per Goldman Sachs), and the IMF finds stablecoin use relative to GDP is highest in Latin America and the Caribbean, followed by Africa and the Middle East — the very regions China's e-CNY is trying to court.
insights.speaker: Agathe Demarais
insights.so_what: China's target markets for e-CNY adoption are already being captured by dollar stablecoins — a specific, addressable competitive threat to Beijing's digital-currency ambitions.
insights.locator: Such digital coins are most popular

insights.claim: Russia is the clearest case that Chinese financial tools gain adoption only under compulsion: Moscow embraced the renminbi and Russian banks joined CIPS en masse only after Western sanctions eliminated their alternatives, and even now no Russian bank is a direct CIPS participant.
insights.speaker: Agathe Demarais
insights.so_what: Predicts that broad non-Western adoption of Chinese financial rails will likely require another sanctions shock (e.g., following a Taiwan contingency) rather than organic demand.
insights.quote: not a single Russian bank is a direct participant in CIPS.
insights.locator: Russia is the best illustration of this

insights.claim: China's three de-dollarization pillars — renminbi trade settlement, CIPS, and e-CNY — share a common flaw: Beijing can build financial instruments at will, but it cannot decree demand for them; absent a massive shock like new Western sanctions, global partners are unlikely to embrace Chinese financial channels.
insights.speaker: Agathe Demarais
insights.so_what: The core forward-looking call: expect continued Chinese institution-building with limited near-term reserve-currency or settlement-share gains for the renminbi, barring a geopolitical shock.
insights.locator: Chinese planners can build as many institutions

insights.claim: Beijing's three financial 'bricks' (trade settlement, CIPS, e-CNY) all illustrate that building instruments is one thing, but fostering demand for them is an entirely different endeavor.
insights.speaker: Agathe Demarais
insights.so_what: A reusable framework for evaluating any state-driven financial-infrastructure push (supply-side capacity vs. organic demand) — applicable beyond China to CBDC efforts elsewhere.
insights.quote: Building instruments is one thing, but fostering demand for them is an entirely different endeavor.
insights.locator: The contrasting fortunes of the e-CNY and dollar-pegged stablecoins
